ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for steamed chicken drumstick rice; mapped to the closest parent class 'cooked rice dish' (FOODON_00002809) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked rice dishes — mixed rice dish with meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (mixed rice dish with meat) used as the closest defensible category; no exact code exists for steamed chicken drumstick rice in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising one steamed chicken drumstick (about 120–130 g) served over a portion of steamed white rice (about 200–220 g), as typically plated at Singaporean hawker centres and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Chicken Drumstick Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Steamed Chicken Drumstick Rice (cr_id G00010697). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for steamed chicken drumstick (USDA #05066) combined with cooked white rice (USDA #20045), proportioned to match the HPB dish composition, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Steamed Chicken Drumstick Rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the GI of white rice (jasmine/long-grain, cooked), which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor in this dish, as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 44.6) / 100 ≈ 32.1, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2 g total carbohydrate; rice component carbohydrate contribution estimated at 44.6 g (200 g cooked rice × 22.3 g carb/100 g per USDA SR Legacy #20045); GL = (72 × 44.6) / 100 = 32.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management; individuals should avoid adding soy-based dipping sauces, remove chicken skin to reduce saturated fat, and limit portion size to keep total sodium intake within the 2,000 mg/day target recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI ≈ 72) contributing 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g with significant glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index of approximately 72, producing a substantial glycaemic load per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reduce portion size to 150–180 g cooked rice, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g from chicken skin and cooking), sodium 231 mg/100 g, and refined carbohydrate load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steaming is a heart-favourable preparation method that avoids added oils; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should remove the chicken skin to reduce saturated fat intake, avoid soy-based dipping sauces to limit sodium, and consider replacing white rice with a lower-GI whole grain to reduce atherogenic refined carbohydrate load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Steaming avoids AGE generation; chicken is lean poultry with low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steaming avoids the advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated by high-heat cooking methods; ginger commonly used in the marinade provides anti-inflammatory gingerols, and chicken drumstick is a lean poultry protein; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should remove the skin, minimise soy-based dipping sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and moderate white rice portions to reduce glycaemic-driven systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Chicken Drumstick Rice (蒸鸡腿饭; Nasi Ayam Drumstick Kukus) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of one whole chicken drumstick that has been marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, ginger, sesame oil, and aromatics, then steamed until tender, and served over a portion of steamed white rice. The dish is a staple at Singaporean food courts and hawker centres, commonly available at lunch and dinner. The steaming method preserves the natural juices of the chicken, resulting in moist, tender meat with a mild savoury flavour and a clean, light texture compared to roasted or fried alternatives. The rice absorbs some of the chicken drippings and marinade, adding subtle umami depth. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Potassium is notably high at 327 mg/100 g, and dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g is moderate. Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (GI ≈ 72), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. Steaming as a cooking method avoids the generation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with high-heat cooking, making this dish one of the healthier preparations of chicken rice available in the Singaporean hawker context. The dish is culturally rooted in Chinese-Singaporean cuisine and is considered a lighter, health-conscious alternative to Hainanese chicken rice or roasted chicken rice.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010697, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy proportional composition. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, which is a well-established analogous estimate for rice-based dishes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for steamed chicken drumstick (USDA #05066) and cooked white rice (USDA #20045), proportioned to match the HPB dish composition, as the HPB Foodo entry does not report saturated fat separately. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010697, and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central to provide these values with defensible confidence. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry. Note that chicken drumstick does contain cholesterol in reality; this value may reflect a data entry limitation in the HPB source — users should be aware that actual cholesterol content of a chicken drumstick serving is likely 60–90 mg per 100 g of chicken meat. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (jasmine/long-grain, cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 350 g serving size and rice-component carbohydrate method. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for steamed chicken drumstick rice as a named dish. 6. Aliases consistency self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language (english: 'Steamed Chicken Drumstick Rice', 'Steamed Chicken Leg Rice', 'Steamed Drumstick Rice', 'Steamed Chicken Rice with Drumstick'; chinese: '蒸鸡腿饭', '蒸雞腿飯'; malay: 'Nasi Ayam Drumstick Kukus', 'Nasi Ayam Kukus'; tamil: 'வேகவைத்த கோழி தொடை சோறு'; hindi: 'भाप में पकी चिकन ड्रमस्टिक चावल'; romanized: 'Zheng Ji Tui Fan', 'Zhēng jī tuǐ fàn') verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. All 12 entries confirmed present.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from HPB source data. Ontology match types set to 'approximate' — flagged for correction.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'. Added missing 'Zhēng jī tuǐ fàn' to flat aliases array. Hindi entry in aliases_by_language contained a mixed-script typo ('ड्रumस्टिक') that did not match the flat aliases entry ('ड्रमस्टिक') — this caused the q8 gate failure reported by the validator.

Iteration 3: Corrected the hindi alias in aliases_by_language from 'भाप में पकी चिकन ड्रumस्टिक चावल' (mixed Latin/Devanagari) to 'भाप में पकी चिकन ड्रमस्टिक चावल' (full Devanagari) to match the flat aliases array verbatim. Performed full aliases consistency self-check across all 12 alias strings. All entries now verified consistent between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array.
